items.title: Landing a Big Tech Internship as a UCLA student
items.description: Internship recruiting is a rite of passage for every computer science student. Each year brings the same familiar cycle: grinding through endless LeetCode problems, submitting hundreds of applications, and constantly refreshing your inbox in hopes of a reply. As a rising third-year computer science major at UCLA, I’ve experienced my fair share of rejections and final-round disappointments. This summer, I’m incredibly lucky to be interning at Apple as a software engineer in Los Angeles 😄 The road here was far from smooth. In this post, I’ll break down how I approached recruiting, what I wish I’d done differently, and what I think actually makes a difference when it comes to landing an internship. I’ll also share my experience studying computer science at UCLA, plus advice for future and incoming Bruins on how to make the most of their time here. Feel free to jump ahead! For those of you who are just starting your internship search, here are some of the most important points that I wish I knew as an incoming freshman. Most large companies receive so many applications that they rely on Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS) and, more recently, AI to scan and rank resumes before a human ever sees them. If your resume lacks the right keywords, formatting, or even the right school, it might not be reviewed at all. If your resume does make it past the software, the first human to read it will likely be a technical recruiter. However, despite “technical” being in the job title, these recruiters do not always have a technical background and may not fully understand the details of your projects or experience. In many cases, they are more familiar with industry buzzwords than technical concepts and will skim resumes quickly, often spending just 20 to 30 seconds scanning for well-known company names and standout bullet points. Now, you might be thinking: “Why not just fill my resume with a bunch of buzzwords to catch their attention?” The problem is that the next person to read your resume will probably be an engineer with a strong technical background. They will be able to spot any exaggerated claims or buzzwords, and anything you include on your resume is fair game in an interview. Writing your resume for three separate audiences can be challenging, and it’s crucial to understand this from the start. There are a couple of reasons why applying early really matters. First, most companies review applications on a rolling basis. This means that the earlier you apply, the better your chances of being considered for the role. Second, internship postings get flooded with applicants within hours. Most students look for roles in the same places: LinkedIn and a few shared GitHub repositories that track internship openings. These sources are updated daily, and thousands of students are monitoring them, so roles posted there can fill up fast. Companies typically post internships starting as early as the summer before the role starts, with recruiting lasting into the following spring. To stay competitive, start early and check postings regularly. From personal experience, I’ve seen postings close before I even had a chance to apply, and a few where I genuinely believe I would have gotten a first-round interview if I had just applied earlier. Realizing you missed the window can be one of the more frustrating parts of internship recruiting, so it’s very important to apply as soon as things drop. You will often see people brag about landing an offer from a single cold message. While networking can be incredibly helpful, it won’t magically open doors for you unless you really put in the effort. Networking tends to fall into two main categories: (1) career exploration and (2) finding job opportunities. The first is about talking to people, learning what they do, and understanding how they got there. The second is more goal-oriented and involves looking for referrals or potential openings. Since coming to UCLA, I’ve had dozens of one-on-one calls with engineers, managers, and recruiters from various companies. These conversations have been incredibly helpful for learning about different career paths, company cultures, and what recruiters look for in candidates. If you’re just getting started with recruiting, setting up these kinds of chats can be a great way to ease into the process. Many of these conversations have also helped me secure referrals and even interviews later on. Most large tech companies allow employee referrals, and the people you talk to will often be willing to help (keep in mind that referrals at larger companies hold less weight due to the volume of candidates). Cold messaging can be especially effective when reaching out to startups where the hiring process is more personal. I haven’t personally tried this approach, but I’ve heard numerous success stories from others who have. One challenge you might run into with networking is that not many people have the time or are interested in responding to cold messages. Out of the hundreds of cold messages I’ve sent on LinkedIn and email, only a few dozen replied, and even fewer were willing to chat. While this is completely understandable, it can make networking feel like a numbers game at times. Alternatively, I’ve had much more success reaching out to family friends, friends of friends, or even more distant connections to get introductions. One final point: there are plenty of networking “hacks” people use to improve their chances of landing an offer. One strategy I’ve seen is finding the recruiter assigned to your school and reaching out to them directly. Others have asked their referrers to personally vouch for them to the hiring manager, rather than just submitting a resume through the referral portal. It might take a bit of extra effort, but these kinds of approaches can help get your application in front of the right eyes and ultimately make a big difference. My number one piece of advice for students looking for their first internship is to build projects. If you don’t have a lot of experience on your resume, side projects are the best way to show off your skills and initiative. Projects are valuable because they make great talking points in interviews and show that you can apply what you’ve learned outside of the classroom. Recruiters will often look for a dedicated projects section on your resume, so having solid examples is arguably even more important than your GPA or what classes you’ve taken. Building something on your own can feel intimidating at first, but anyone with some basic coding experience is capable of doing it. There are plenty of guides and starter ideas online to help you get going. If you need somewhere to start, I have even put together workshop curriculum on full-stack development that will give you all the skills you need to make your first project. If building independent projects isn’t something you enjoy, college is the perfect time to join clubs or meet people who can help you work on group projects instead. At the end of the day, the most important thing is that you’re building projects and showing why a company should hire you. Online Assessments, or OAs, are take-home technical assessments that companies use to evaluate an applicant’s coding abilities. Some companies send OAs to every applicant, others only send them to applicants that pass an initial resume screen, and some may skip them altogether. For most companies, OAs will consist of a couple “LeetCode” style questions that have to be solved within a certain time constraint (usually around 1 hour). Unless you have experience with competitive programming, you will have to study a good amount (think 3+ months) to get a handle on the type of problems that might be thrown at you. Because internships are so competitive, companies often use the OA stage to quickly filter out candidates. Anything short of a perfect score can hurt your chances, so it’s important to be comfortable solving these kinds of problems under time pressure. Beyond the OA, knowing how to solve LeetCode questions is crucial because you will often be asked the same type of questions in technical interviews. However, keep in mind that they’re evaluated very differently. In an OA, your score is typically based on how many test cases your code passes. That means your solution needs to be fully correct and functional. In a technical interview, the focus is more on your thought process and how clearly you can explain your approach. In many cases, you’re not even expected to write fully working code. Both OAs and technical interviews can be challenging in different ways, so it’s important to practice and build your confidence for each. The reality of searching for a software job in 2025 is that the school you attend will significantly impact your chances of being considered at every stage of the recruitment process. While each company approaches recruiting differently, many maintain a short list of schools they actively recruit from. One manager I talked to put it bluntly: “Stanford, CMU, Berkeley, UW, and GT resumes go on the top. UCLA resumes might end up in the middle. Anything below that won’t even be considered.” This is clearly an extreme example. Plenty of students from non-target schools still land internships at Big Tech companies, but it’s a useful way to set your expectations going into the process. If you go to a non-target school, you’ll likely have to put more effort into applying, networking, and building your resume to stay competitive. Companies prefer to hire third-year students because they’ve usually taken more classes, have more experience, and are just one year away from being eligible for full-time roles. Many companies will filter out first and second year students during the initial resume screen, so listing your actual graduation date as a freshman or sophomore can hurt your chances. Even if you do make it to the interview stage, having a later grad date can still work against you. Many companies are strict about only hiring students who are on track to graduate within a year or two. In some cases, your grad date can even be the deciding factor between you and another equally qualified candidate. If you’ve tried recruiting as a first or second year student, you’ve probably heard of early career programs like Google STEP or Microsoft Explore. These internships are designed for freshmen and sophomores, and they’re often hyped as some of the best opportunities for younger students. However, what often gets overlooked is that these programs primarily target students from historically underrepresented backgrounds in tech. It’s great that these programs exist, but if you don’t fall into one of those groups, your chances of being considered are close to zero. This is just something to keep in mind so you can set realistic expectations before applying. You’ve probably heard the cliché: “C’s get degrees.” That might be true, but C’s probably won’t get you a good job. 3.5 is often tossed around as the benchmark GPA to stay competitive, but there’s really no magic number. Especially at top programs, CS classes can be tough and curves aren’t always generous. Most companies understand this and won’t automatically filter you out for a lower GPA. Of course, the closer to 4.0 the better, but a few points difference won’t make a meaningful difference (unless you want to pursue a more competitive field like quant for example). There are also a few good reasons not to obsess over a perfect GPA. First, the stress and workload required to get A’s in every class can be overwhelming. Even if you got straight A’s in high school, doing the same in college is a completely different challenge. Second, the extra time and energy it takes to chase perfect grades is often better spent elsewhere, including living your life and touching grass. From what I’ve seen, recruiters care far more about what you’ve actually done than a few extra points on your GPA. Your past experience, the projects you’ve built, the skills you’ve developed, and to a lesser extent, the classes you’ve taken all carry more weight. When people ask me what to prioritize when trying to land their first internship, I always give the same advice: focus on building projects and learning new skills. Doing well in your classes is great, but it won’t mean much if you’re not doing anything outside the classroom. Internship recruiting can be incredibly frustrating, especially when the process feels unclear and the results seem out of your control. There have been plenty of times where I felt like I did everything right, from the OA to the final interview, and still didn’t get the offer. And other times, I didn’t do anything special but somehow ended up with a lucky opportunity. My best advice for handling the ups and downs of recruiting is to avoid dwelling on things that are out of your control and instead focus on the opportunities that are still out there. Chances are, you’ll be affected by headcount or stack ranking at some point. It can be incredibly frustrating, but it’s not something to take personally. I’ve gotten a lot of questions from UCLA computer science students about what they can do to get ahead. Most of my answers can be condensed into a few areas. In my opinion, your major doesn’t matter all that much if your goal is to land a software role. What really matters is the classes you take. As long as you take a few select CS classes (32, 35L, 111, 180), you should be equipped to recruit for software roles regardless of your major. CS-adjacent majors (EE, CE, CSE, Math of Comp, Ling + CS, etc.) will have no problem taking these core CS courses and will even have the opportunity to take some of the same electives as CS majors. Engineering majors especially have a lot of flexibility through tech breadth and major electives to fit these courses in. One question you might have if you’re majoring in something other than CS is whether recruiters will perceive your application differently. From my experience, it’s not a major issue. Your major likely won’t have a significant impact on your chances, and many UCLA students tend to fudge their major a bit anyway (e.g., Math of Comp → Computer Science and Math, Ling and CS → Computer Science and Linguistics). I know plenty of people who are sticking with a CS-adjacent major and still planning on going into software post-grad. As long as you’ve taken the right classes and have relevant experience, your major matters a lot less than you might think. There are a few classes at UCLA that are definitely worth taking, either for the experience they provide or how they look on a resume: I often get asked whether to take class X or class Y and what quarter is best for each class. The truth is that most of these questions need to be answered on a case-by-case basis. Oftentimes, the answer really depends on what classes you have already taken, the professor teaching the class, and what electives you plan on taking. To get an idea of what classes you might take in your first two years, here’s every class I’ve taken so far: UCLA’s CS major is fairly long, given the number of lower-division math, physics, and CS classes required. Out of all the lower-div classes I’ve taken, I found the math classes to be the easiest by far. The physics classes varied a lot in difficulty depending on the professor, and the CS classes were manageable overall (with the exception of CS 35L, which is notoriously challenging). One other thing to point out is that I only ended up taking one major-related class in Winter 25. That wasn’t intentional. I just couldn’t get the classes I wanted because of my enrollment time. At a large public university like UCLA, it is pretty common for classes to fill up before you get a chance to enroll. Finally, if you’re an incoming freshman and want to get ahead, one way to prepare is by giving yourself a crash course on C++ (the first two CS courses you’ll take are both in C++). If you want more structure, you can probably find a CS 31 course website from a previous quarter with homework assignments and projects. It can also be helpful to take a class or two at a community college before starting at UCLA. Many community colleges offer courses that can fulfill lower-div math and physics requirements ( it’s usually harder to transfer CS credits). Coming in with extra units can improve your enrollment time and may even help you graduate early. Getting involved in a professional-focused student organization early on is one of the most valuable things you can do as a new student at UCLA. In my experience, clubs have opened up a ton of opportunities I wouldn’t have found otherwise and also introduced me to a good chunk of my friends. Unlike in high school, clubs in college can have competitive recruitment processes. My advice is to take club recruitment seriously as a freshman: show up to EAF (the club fair during Week 0), attend info sessions, apply broadly, put yourself out there as much as possible, and do research on each organization to boost your chances. Here are some of the larger CS-related clubs at UCLA: There are also several CS-adjacent clubs that involve a lot of software work and can offer strong project experience. I won’t go into as much detail about these, but they are worth checking out: Clubs can be very competitive, and it’s easy to feel frustrated if things don’t go your way. I’ve been rejected from plenty of clubs myself, and it’s never fun knowing that another student decided what you can and can’t be part of. That said, UCLA has a huge range of student organizations, some of which are very open and not competitive at all, and you’ll eventually find your community if you put in the effort. I’ve covered a lot in this article, but there’s still plenty I didn’t get into. At the end of the day, there’s no single roadmap for landing an internship, but I hope the points I shared here give you a solid starting point. If you’re an incoming Bruin looking for a more detailed take on UCLA computer science, I also wrote this article last year that dives deeper into that (though my opinions of UCLA have definitely changed a bit since then). If you’re just getting ready to apply for your first internship, good luck!

items.title: UCLA Computer Science: Reflections from a First-Year Student
items.description: As my freshman year at UCLA concludes, I’m excited to share my initial thoughts on being a student here, specifically what it’s like to study computer science. This article delves into my experiences with coursework, extracurricular activities, and the engineering culture at UCLA. I really enjoyed my first year at UCLA, but there are a few things I wish I had known and prepared for before starting school here. In this article, I offer some tips and observations, such as strategies for course registration and club involvement, to help future Bruins navigate their own UCLA journey. One of the main differences between UCLA computer science and computer science programs at other large public universities is its emphasis on breadth. During my freshman year, I took a mix of math, physics, and computer science classes. Here’s what my schedule looked like: Most engineering students take three or four core classes per quarter, sometimes adding a lab or general education requirement. All of the classes I’ve taken, except for Sociology 1, are lower-division requirements for the computer science major. Looking back at my first year, I would say that the most memorable academic experience was taking the Physics 1 series. All UCLA computer science majors are required to take the Physics 1 series, which is notorious for being a GPA crusher. This requirement is quite unusual, as most computer science programs at other universities typically only require one physics class. At UCLA, however, computer science students must complete three physics classes in addition to a lab. Personally, I found Physics 1A and Physics 1B to be my most challenging courses this past year. Despite taking AP Physics C: Mechanics in high school, I still struggled with the higher level of difficulty in Physics 1A. Later, I found out that you can actually petition the engineering department to bypass Physics 1A with a score of 4 or 5 on the AP Mechanics exam. Physics 1B proved to be even more challenging. Not only was it completely new material (I hate circuits), but our professor was new, gave out weekly quizzes, and made the exams extremely complicated. Luckily, the class was single-handedly saved by our TA (god bless Rayner). As a first-year student, it’s important to recognize that many lower-division requirements, such as the Physics 1 series, are “weeder” classes known for their rigor and grade deflation. However, the difficulty of these classes can also vary based on the professor and the class cycle. Early on, I discovered how a good professor can transform a challenging class into a bearable (and sometimes even enjoyable) experience. Like many schools, UCLA has its own professor rating website: Bruinwalk. When I found a Math 33B professor known to have a 70% A rate, I rushed to sign up for their class section. Conversely, when I realized the only professor teaching Math 61 had a sub-2 star rating, I opted to take that course a different quarter. Taking classes “on-cycle” versus “off-cycle” can also make a huge difference. Most computer science freshmen choose to take CS 31 starting in fall quarter, CS 32 in winter, and CS 33 in spring — this is considered “on-cycle”. With this in mind, I decided to delay taking these classes by one quarter, not enrolling in CS 31 until winter quarter. Although enrolling “off-cycle” put me a quarter behind other students, it proved beneficial. I encountered more manageable professors and a less rigorous grading curve. While my “on-cycle” friends complained constantly about the workload in their computer science classes, I found CS 31 and CS 32 to be notably less challenging. I probably watched a grand total of three lectures across both classes, though I still spent 30+ hours on the infamous CS 32 project 3. Navigating course registration at UCLA can be daunting. I remember my first time trying to enroll in classes, only to find out that most of my desired courses were already full. Just like other large public universities, UCLA has the problem of having way too many students for the number of spots offered in each class. This means in-demand classes can be very difficult to get, especially for younger students with later enrollment times. Here’s some of the strategies I’ve learned to manage this process better: Beyond what I have already covered, one thing that stood out about UCLA academics is its computer science curriculum. UCLA’s introductory computer science courses are taught in C++, and I would bet that the curriculum hasn’t changed in the last 20 years or so (also evidenced by the class website http://web.cs.ucla.edu/classes/spring24/cs32/syllabus.html this thing looks like it was made in the stone age). Even though learning an aging programming language is not a problem in itself, I would argue that UCLA’s computer science introductory courses are outdated and too conceptual. Whereas my friends at other universities are learning industry-applicable skills and technologies, I am stuck learning how to implement a doubly linked list with a dummy head node. Which is to say not very useful or interesting at all. I know that UCLA’s classes become more practical down the line, but these are some of my observations so far. Outside of coursework, extracurricular activities were an important part of my first year at UCLA. Unlike high school clubs, which are often used as gimmicks for college applications, academic clubs at the university-level are a lot more serious and can be great resume boosters. UCLA has a ton of computer science-focused clubs, from national organizations like ACM and IEEE to smaller groups like Creative Labs and LA Hacks. Two clubs that I am actively involved in are DevX and DataRes Consulting. DevX is a project-focused club where we work in 8–12 member teams to develop full-stack apps. I am currently working on an app called Retune, which is trying to improve music exploration by combining location sharing with Spotify activity. Next year, I will be on the DevX board as developer lead, running our club’s dev intern program. DataRes Consulting is also a project-based club, but we work with startups to provide data science and machine learning consulting services. In spring quarter, I worked on a team developing an AI chatbot for the startup Chelle AI. Being involved in academic clubs has been a great way to explore new technical areas and get more project experience, which is essential for any computer science major. It has also provided opportunities to meet new people, especially students from different grades. With my involvement in social clubs as well, participating in extracurricular activities has become a core and fun part of my UCLA experience. One thing that has always puzzled me is the competitiveness of many clubs. For example, DevX’s sub-5% acceptance rate meant that even highly qualified students face rejection. This level of competition often feels more intense than applying to the university itself. To make things worse, many of these clubs choose to brag about their low acceptance rates and rigorous selection processes. From what I have seen, academic clubs often face one of two problems. 1. Club A has great professional opportunities but is super elitist and difficult to get into. 2. Club B is open to all students but is disorganized and doesn’t end up doing much. ACM is an example of Club B at UCLA. ACM positions itself as a beginner-friendly club catering to a variety of technical interests, with different committees for competitive programming, app development, cloud computing, and more. In theory, this sounds great. In practice, this broad structure can lead to disorganization and resource constraints. For instance, some of my friends in the ACM AI committee were assigned to projects that never got off the ground, while others didn’t get assigned to a project at all. That said, some of my friends had excellent experiences in other ACM committees, like ACM Cyber. While the broad structure of ACM can create hurdles, there are areas within the club where members can find valuable opportunities. What does this mean? It highlights the importance of being proactive and making the most of the resources available. While navigating clubs at UCLA and other universities can be challenging, actively engaging with them can can lead to meaningful experiences and personal growth. Engineering at UCLA, like many large public universities, is heavily male-dominated, especially in computer science. However, one thing that sets UCLA apart is its embrace of engineers with non-traditional backgrounds. While I have met many students like myself who have long known they wanted to study engineering, I have also encountered quite a few who hadn’t decided on engineering until committing to UCLA. For example, a friend of mine spent most of high school focusing on pre-health extracurriculars. After being accepted to UCLA for electrical engineering, they pivoted to EE despite having no prior experience in the field. This means the range of students studying engineering at UCLA is quite diverse. In each of my classes, for every top student — the super-genius curve breakers that I would struggle to keep up with even if I tried — there are two students who are just beginning to navigate this level of coursework. While students at UCLA are still heavily academically inclined, the culture here is not as competitive or rigorous as I expected One of the reasons I chose UCLA over other universities was its promise of a fun college experience, and it has not disappointed. I’ve been pleasantly surprised to find that even among engineering students, maintaining a healthy work-life balance is highly valued here. UCLA is home to a huge athletic culture, where playing sports and hitting the gym is common — even amongst nerds. People love to go out and eat. The beach is only a few miles away. As a tennis player, living in LA has been a particular treat. With the second-largest tennis tournament in the U.S. just a couple of hours away, I’ve had the opportunity to watch top tennis pros practice right on our UCLA courts (as well as trek out to the tournament itself). With so many things nearby, living in LA is a lot of fun, making it one of the major perks of attending UCLA. With so many things nearby, living in LA is a lot of fun, making it one of the major perks of attending UCLA. While I appreciate a healthy work-life balance, I’ve noticed that career-focused motivation is frequently underemphasized within the engineering community. Since arriving at UCLA, I haven’t experienced a significant push to further my professional ambitions, and much of the career-oriented work I’ve done has been self-initiated. UCLA’s career resources do not provide an environment conducive to advancing careers in engineering and technology. The career center offers minimal help, and anyone who has attended a UCLA career fair knows the frustration of waiting in long lines without even getting a chance to speak one-on-one with a recruiter. From what I’ve heard, the career center does a poor job of fostering connections with tech companies, making UCLA even less of a recruiting hotspot for engineering. To make matters worse, UCLA is not a target school for computer science. While our program is relatively highly ranked (top 15), it places us behind top-tier programs like Stanford, Berkeley, and the University of Washington. As a result, UCLA students lose a competitive edge when it comes to tech recruitment. In my experience, UCLA’s alumni network is also limited in the support it provides. Although I’ve managed to arrange some coffee chats and secure a few referrals from former Bruins, the majority of alumni don’t seem interested in offering assistance. Compared to a school like USC, which is renowned for its strong alumni network, UCLA’s network is not as competitive. Not to say that it is impossible for computer science students at UCLA to be successful. A quick scroll on LinkedIn reveals plenty of UCLA students with FAANG+ internships. There are still incredibly motivated individuals at UCLA, but the overall environment doesn’t seem to foster a high level of career-focused ambition. If there is one thing UCLA does well, it is making research opportunities accessible to self-motivated students. Every one of my friends who tried to join a research lab managed to secure a research position by cold emailing professors (unfortunately, the official research portal is unreliable). Often, this research ends up being tangential to their career goals, such as doing bioinformatics as a data science major. While career-focused students may be disappointed by UCLA’s lack of resources, self-motivated students will still find plenty of opportunities here, whether that be through clubs, research, or local startups. UCLA might not be as competitive or demanding as a school like Berkeley, but its emphasis on work-life balance can help students excel in other areas. Quick little tidbit about switching majors: Transferring to computer science is common and doable, which is great news for students like me who didn’t initially apply to the School of Engineering. All you need is a 3.5 GPA, an engineering workload (taking a science class and a math class in the same quarter), and completion of CS 31, 32, and 33. As I reflect on my freshman year, I’m grateful for the opportunities and challenges that have shaped my journey so far. Attending college has been a huge wake-up call in terms of time and priority management, and UCLA has definitely helped me become more independent. While I still take great care to excel in my academics, I also make sure to stay involved with both academic and social clubs. Despite the demands of my part-time internship at Lockheed, I make it a point to take every opportunity to go out and enjoy myself. I continue to prioritize side projects and learning new technical skills while also emphasizing the importance of staying physically active. This year, I’ve made tons of friends and gotten to play way too much poker, leaving me with very little to complain about. While I have some qualms with the career resources here, I believe UCLA offers a fantastic environment for self-motivated students to thrive. The diverse experiences and challenges at UCLA have made my freshman year incredibly memorable. I’m very excited to return in the fall and see what the next three years will bring.

items.title: My Experience as a High School Intern at Lockheed Martin
items.description: Every high school student knows the stress of lining up summer opportunities, whether that be a job, volunteer work, a research position, or even that coveted internship. For those looking for a summer internship, you have probably already run into the qualification predicament. For most companies, a high school education is not enough for the job; most are looking for college students, specifically those from top universities. But even if they don’t, they’re looking for candidates with experience, and most high school students don’t fit the bill. Just like tons of other high school students, I asked myself “how can I get an internship if it requires experience that I can only get from this internship?” I need experience to get experience, but I can’t get that experience without prior experience. How does that even work? Well, first of all, that is not necessarily true. But second of all, networking and a lot of luck. I am now an incoming freshman at UCLA, but I have been interning at Lockheed Martin since the summer after junior year of high school. So 15 months and counting. And while I didn’t get my internship through the grind of cold email networking en masse like some others, my relationship with my AP Computer Science teacher helped point me in the right direction. As part of the computer science club at my high school, I was able to participate in the annual Codequest competition held by Lockheed Martin. Now if you’re familiar with competitive programming, Codequest is just another regional competitive programming competition with teams representing different high schools. And even though my team got its ass kicked (even with a USACO plat + gold on our team), the competition offered an internship application for all participants. The really great thing about this opportunity with Lockheed Martin was that they were looking for high school applicants specifically. So when it came to the interview, they cared a lot more about why I was interested in a technical field (for me computer science) and how I had pursued it (intellectual vitality) rather than previous industry experience (phew!). Luckily for me, I had plenty to talk about due to a variety of personal projects I had been working on for a few years prior. While these projects were nothing impressive on their own, they gave me an opportunity to show that I was invested in learning different aspects of computer science. My experience working on my own projects really helped carry me through that interview, ultimately landing me an internship offer later that week. Just some tidbits about the job itself. I am working remotely as is the rest of my team. Most of the people I work with live on the east coast, and the three hour time difference has proven to be difficult at times. There was also a 3 week period where I had to attend a 5am-10am training which was a bit of a pain (the training leaders were based in India I believe). My team is on a 4/10 work schedule, but it is not always expected to work the full 40 hours a week as an intern. There is lots of flex time based on the amount of work, which is really convenient. Also, I am the only intern on the team which is kind of interesting. Probably the most difficult part of this internship was getting started. I was not told my start date until the night before, and my work computer was randomly shipped to me with no prior notice. My manager also had no idea that I was in a different time zone, and I remember receiving multiple calls on the first day at 5ish in the morning (I was still sleeping). But after this initial lapse of communication, the logistics of the internship were laid out well, and I had no related issues going forward. Technically speaking, my work computer was a pain to set up, but that probably goes with most jobs. I also remember having a severe case of imposter syndrome getting started. In fact, after just a few days, I remember telling myself that I would not make it through the summer. And to be honest, the first few weeks were incredibly overwhelming. My team threw loads of information my way, from different documentation sources to a heap of development processes and softwares, all of which I spent over a hundred hours poring through. Although the start was difficult, my “buddy” on the team was a world of help to me. I learned quickly to ask a ton of questions, schedule meetings with the team, and ask for demos and examples if I needed help. Being able to communicate difficulties I was having and asking questions really paid dividends for me. At this point, I have been working at Lockheed Martin for over a year on a few different projects, and I feel a lot more confident. For those interested in the actual work I am doing, here’s a few technical tidbits: Year 1: Java API development, Postman testing, GraphQL API development, API documentation automated generation Year 2: eQubeMI development (eQube is a no/low-code software solution), Javascript API testing Overall, I have been really happy about my internship experience at Lockheed Martin. It has been a great learning experience for me, and I really enjoy working with my team. The one qualm I might have is the pay; many of my friends end up earning a bit (sometimes a lot) more working other jobs, though they do not have the same resume value and experience potential as this internship. A worthy trade-off. For anyone looking to follow this path, the first step is joining or even starting the Codequest team at your school. This probably means getting involved with your school’s computer science/STEM related club (something you should do either way!). While I would highly recommend finding an internship like this, there are certainly other great opportunities I would consider. The following is a short list of summer activities that I have heard other students pursue: 1. Research position. This can be either through a program (there are tons for high school students) or through a university. Finding a professor to research under is definitely a more difficult approach but can also pay dividends. Great learning experience! 2. Paid job. Making some money over the summer can never hurt on top of gaining invaluable work experience. This can be related to one of your interests or not. 3. Volunteer work. Lots of opportunities out there, can be either in your immediate community or have a larger scope. Never hurts to be related to one of your interests. 4. Summer programs. There are a ton of different programs (many are paid) through various universities. Find a program related to one of your interests for maximum benefit. 5. Summer classes. Many students end up taking classes to get ahead in high school, knock out some GE requirements, or learn something they are interested in. Most classes are low-cost. Whatever you decide to do with your summer is not meant to be overly stressful, though it may seem that way sometimes. Good luck!
